title: The Perceptron Learning Rule
concept: When a perceptron guesses wrong, the perceptron learning rule nudges each weight up or down based on the size of the error, so that over many examples its guesses get better and better.

exercises.prompt: Recap: A perceptron fires only when its weighted sum does what?
exercises.explain: Right! Weighted sum vs. threshold is the whole firing decision. But where do the weights themselves come from? Today: how a perceptron learns them.

cards.title: What happens when the guess is wrong?
cards.body: So far, we've assumed a perceptron already has the "right" weights. But at the very start, its weights are just random guesses — and those guesses will often be wrong. So what happens the moment a perceptron gets an answer wrong?

cards.title: What is the perceptron learning rule?
cards.body: The perceptron learning rule is a simple correction: after every guess, compare the perceptron's output to the correct answer. If it guessed too low, nudge the relevant weights up a little; if it guessed too high, nudge them down. Repeat this over many examples, and the weights gradually settle into ones that get the answer right.

cards.title: Like a coach correcting a free-throw shooter
cards.body: A coach watching a player miss a free throw to the left doesn't rebuild the player from scratch — they give one small correction: "aim a little more right." Miss again, another small nudge. Over many shots, the small corrections add up into a reliably accurate shooter. A perceptron's weights get nudged the exact same way, shot after shot.

cards.title: One nudge, step by step
cards.body: Say a perceptron sees an example where the correct output is 1, but with its current weight it only outputs 0 — its guess was too low. The learning rule says: increase the weight tied to that input. If instead it wrongly output 1 when the correct answer was 0, the rule says: decrease that weight. Right guess, no error, no change at all.

cards.title: One mistake doesn't fix everything
cards.body: It's tempting to think a perceptron learns "the rule" after seeing just one wrong example. It doesn't — one nudge is a small, partial correction, not a final answer. A perceptron needs to see the same examples many times over (each full pass is often called an epoch) before its weights settle into ones that get every example right.

cards.title: Wrong Guess vs. Right Guess
cards.body: What happens to the weights after each example:

left.text:
Output ≠ correct answer
Weights get nudged
A small correction is made

right.text:
Output = correct answer
Weights stay exactly the same
Nothing needs fixing

exercises.prompt: According to the perceptron learning rule, what happens when the perceptron's guess is already correct?

options.text: Nothing — the weights are left unchanged
The weights are reset to zero

exercises.explain: Right — if the guess already matches the correct answer, there's no error to correct, so the weights stay exactly as they are.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A perceptron typically needs to see the same training examples multiple times before its weights settle into correct values.
exercises.explain: True! A single pass rarely fixes everything — perceptrons usually need many repeated passes (epochs) through the examples to converge on correct weights.

exercises.sentence: If a perceptron's output is too low compared to the correct answer, the learning rule nudges the relevant weights ___.
exercises.explain: When the guess is too low, weights get nudged up, making the perceptron more likely to fire next time on a similar input.

exercises.prompt: Which of these best describes the perceptron learning rule in action?

options.text: After each wrong guess, weights get a small nudge up or down based on the error; right guesses leave weights unchanged
After every single example, all weights are thrown out and replaced with brand new random numbers
The perceptron learns everything perfectly the very first time it sees any example

exercises.explain: Exactly — small, error-based nudges, repeated over many examples, are what let a perceptron's weights converge on correct values.

exercises.prompt: A perceptron with threshold 2 sees an input where the correct answer is 1, but its current weights only produce a weighted sum of 1 — not enough to fire. What should the perceptron learning rule do next?

options.text: Increase the weights tied to that example's active inputs, so the weighted sum is more likely to cross the threshold next time
Immediately lower the threshold to 0 so anything at all fires
Leave the weights untouched, since the guess was close enough
Discard the example entirely and never learn from it

exercises.explain: Correct! Since the guess was too low, the learning rule nudges the relevant weights up. Repeat this correction across a full truth table, and a perceptron can learn to reproduce an entire logic gate — starting next lesson, with AND.
